title: Charmed Life - Chapter 5 (Fragment 4)
text:
“I don’t know yet,” said Gwendolen. “But I think I might. I’m on Advanced Magic, you know. And I want dragons’ blood in case I need it.”

“It’ll come dear,” Mr. Baslam warned her. “It’s costly stuff. There’s the risk to pay for, you see. I don’t want the law on me.”

“I can pay,” said Gwendolen. “I’ll pay in installments. You can take the rest of the five shillings on account.”

Mr. Baslam was unable to resist this. The way he looked at the crown piece Gwendolen handed to him made Cat see vividly a long row of frothing pints of beer. “Done,” said Mr. Baslam. Gwendolen smiled graciously and got up to go. Cat thankfully leaped up too. “What about you, young gentleman?” Mr. Baslam asked wheedlingly. “Aren’t you going to try your hand at a bit of necromancy at all?”

“He’s just my brother,” said Gwendolen.

“Oh. Ah. Um. Yes,” said Mr. Baslam. “He’s that one, of course. Well, good day to you both. Come again, any time.”

“When will you have the dragons’ blood?” Gwendolen asked him on the doorstep.

Mr. Baslam thought. “Say a week?”

Gwendolen’s face glowed. “How quick! I knew you were a good agent. Where do you get it from so quickly?”

“Now that would be telling, wouldn’t it?” said Mr. Baslam. “It has to come from another world, but which one is a trade secret, young lady.”

Gwendolen was jubilant as they went back along the alley. “A week!” she said. “That’s the quickest I’ve ever heard. It has to be smuggled in from this other world, you know. He must have awfully good connections there.”

“Or he’s got some already, inside a stuffed bird,” said Cat, who had not liked Mr. Baslam at all. “Whatever do you want dragons’ blood for? Mrs. Sharp says it costs fifty pounds an ounce.”

“Be quiet,” said Gwendolen. “Oh, quick! Hurry, Cat! Get into that sweet shop. She mustn’t know where I’ve been.”

Out on the village green, a lady carrying a parasol was talking to a clergyman. She was Chrestomanci’s wife. Cat and Gwendolen bundled themselves into the shop and hoped she had not seen them. There, Cat bought them a bag of tof-fee each. Millie was still there, so he bought some licorice too. Millie was still talking to the clergyman even then, so he bought Gwendolen a pen wiper and himself a postcard of the Castle. Millie was still there. But Cat could not think of anything else to buy, so they had to come out of the shop.

Millie beckoned to them as soon as they did. “Come and meet the dear vicar.”

The vicar, who was old, with a weak and wandering look, shakily shook hands with them and said he would see them on Sunday. Then he said he really must be going now.

“And so must we,” said Millie. “Come on, my dears. We’ll walk back to the Castle together.”

There was nothing to do but walk beside her under the shadow of her parasol, across the green and between the lodge gates. Cat was afraid she was going to ask them why they had been visiting Mr. Baslam. Gwendolen was sure she was going to ask her about the moles in the lawn. But what Millie said was, “I am glad of a chance to talk to you, my loves. I haven’t had a moment to see how you were getting on. Are you all right? Are you finding it very strange?”

“A—a little,” Cat admitted.

“The first few days are always the worst, anywhere,” said Millie. “I’m sure you’ll soon find your way around. And don’t hesitate to use the toys in the playroom if you want. They’re for everyone. Private toys are in one’s own room. How are you liking your rooms?”

translations.ru: счёт, на который можно записать деньги

vocabulary.definition: A record of financial expenditure or a sum kept for payments to be made later.
vocabulary.example: You can take the rest of the five shillings on account.

vocabulary.definition: An imperative phrase telling someone to stop talking or making noise.
vocabulary.example: Be quiet,

vocabulary.definition: To make a gesture with the hand, arm, or head to encourage someone to come nearer or follow.
vocabulary.example: Millie beckoned to them as soon as they did.

vocabulary.definition: A man who is a member of the clergy, especially in Christian churches.
vocabulary.example: She was talking to a clergyman.

vocabulary.definition: Relationships that help someone get things done or obtain goods.
vocabulary.example: He must have awfully good connections there.

vocabulary.definition: Involving great expense; expensive or high in cost.
vocabulary.example: It’s costly stuff.

vocabulary.definition: Forming or covered with a mass of small bubbles; here describing beer with frothy heads.
vocabulary.example: A long row of frothing pints of beer.

vocabulary.definition: Glasses of beer with a foamy top.
vocabulary.example: The crown piece Gwendolen handed to him made Cat see vividly a long row of frothing pints of beer.

vocabulary.definition: In a kind, courteous, and pleasant manner.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen smiled graciously.

vocabulary.definition: To pause before saying or doing something, often due to uncertainty.
vocabulary.example: Don’t hesitate to use the toys in the playroom if you want.

vocabulary.definition: Partial payments of a total amount due, paid over a period of time.
vocabulary.example: I’ll pay in installments.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or expressing great happiness and triumph.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was jubilant as they went back along the alley.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic, especially communication with the dead to predict the future.
vocabulary.example: Are you going to try your hand at a bit of necromancy at all?

vocabulary.definition: A light umbrella used to give shade from the sun.
vocabulary.example: A lady carrying a parasol was talking to a clergyman.

vocabulary.definition: To divide or distribute into portions.
vocabulary.example: The seller parcelled out the goods carefully.

vocabulary.definition: A room provided for children to play in.
vocabulary.example: The toys in the playroom are for everyone.

vocabulary.definition: To withstand the action or effect of something; to refuse to accept something.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam was unable to resist this.

vocabulary.definition: A former British coin and unit of money worth one-twentieth of a pound.
vocabulary.example: You can take the rest of the five shillings on account.

vocabulary.definition: A polite and warm expression by raising the corners of the mouth.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen smiled graciously and got up to go.

vocabulary.definition: Brought into a place secretly and illegally, especially to avoid paying taxes or restrictions.
vocabulary.example: It has to be smuggled in from this other world.

vocabulary.definition: Filled with soft material (or taxidermied, as in a stuffed bird).
vocabulary.example: He’s got some already, inside a stuffed bird.

vocabulary.definition: Information kept confidential to maintain an advantage in business or trade.
vocabulary.example: Which one is a trade secret.

vocabulary.definition: A representative or parish priest in the Church of England or Anglican churches.
vocabulary.example: The vicar shook hands with them.

vocabulary.definition: Going to see a person or place for a short time.
vocabulary.example: Why they had been visiting Mr. Baslam.

vocabulary.definition: Moving about aimlessly; or in this context, a weak, unfocused look.
vocabulary.example: The vicar, old, with a weak and wandering look.

vocabulary.definition: In a coaxing or flattering manner to persuade someone to do something.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam asked wheedlingly.
